paper_title: Clinicopathological characterization of non-guttata corneal endothelial dystrophy in Saudi patients with idiopathic endothelial failure.
raw_limitation_text:
[DISCUSSION]
Endothelial dysfunction or damage is a common cause of corneal decompensation and visual impairment in elderly patients. The patho-etiology for the development of corneal edema is since corneal endothelial cells have limited regenerative capacity, thus they are compensated by the spreading of the existing endothelial cells to cover the posterior aspect of the cornea. This affects the pump function, leading to edema which can alter the spacing of the stromal collagen fibers and reduce the overall corneal transparency. Several studies reported that people older than 40 years old and women have a higher risk of developing corneal endothelial dystrophy. Islam et al. added that the normal range of corneal endothelial cell density at birth is 4000–5000 cells/mm2, but with aging, it declines with the rate of 0.3–0.6%/year to an average of 2000–3000 cells/mm2 in normal adult eyes. Other causes for endothelial cell dysfunction include viral infections such as mumps and cytomegalovirus endothelialitis, in addition to herpetic kerato-uveitis. In our study group, even though no aqueous humor samples were taken to be tested for possible viral etiology, the possibility of herpetic viral infection has been ruled out based on ocular history, clinical examination (with intact corneal sensation), and negative PCR. Interestingly, Ahuja et al. noted that in Fuchs’ endothelial dystrophy (FED), corneal sensitivity usually decreases and remains subnormal even years after endothelial keratoplasty. They attributed the decreased sensitivity, as being secondary, to the loss of sub-basal nerves and nerve abnormal morphology, which may persist after endothelial keratoplasty. At presentation, our patients’ main complaint was decreased vision, which is the hallmark of FED presentation as well. Hatou et al.explained that, as endothelial dystrophy progresses in FED, the endothelial function deteriorates, and corneal edema intensifies leading to a significant decline in visual acuity. The demographics in our cases showed female preponderance with a female to male ratio of 3:2. Similarly, Sarnicola et al. reported that FED has a female to male ratio of 2.5:1, and no specific reasons for this female predilection could be highlighted.

It seems that the onset of the disease in our cases when compared to FED patients, is relatively delayed with a mean age of 67.2 ± 8.7 years while FED might appear earlier; however, in some reports, most of FED patients do not require surgical intervention until their 6th or even 7th decades of life. However, the clinical behavior of the endothelial dysfunction in our cohort of patients seems to be different, and the histopathological and ultrastructural findings have excluded the diagnosis of FED.

The family history of our patients was negative for any similar condition in any of the relatives, thus a peculiar mode of inheritance in relation to this type of endothelial dysfunction was not suspected.

Regarding the clinical associations, none of our patients was wearing a contact lens, and patients with confirmed PEX were excluded. Fontana et al. stated that pathogenesis of PEX is multifactorial. It has been suggested that PEX is a form of elastosis resulting from the increased production of some elastic micro-fibrillar components like fibrillin-1. The systemic origin of the disorder indicates why patients with unilateral involvement express signs of PEX in the unaffected fellow eye later, suggesting that PEX is a generalized disorder with an asymmetrical clinical presentation. Nevertheless, endothelial compromise related to PEX is well documented, but it doesn’t typically present with corneal edema and secondary decompensation without any intervention in phakic eyes, as their alteration and effect on endothelium are not severe unlike endothelial dystrophies where damage is significant. Aoki T et al. in 2020 demonstrated a significant association between the severity of PEX and the low endothelial cell density. They also suggested that the accumulation of PEX material leads to an early endothelial cell dysfunction.

The earliest description of the proposed condition in our study was more than forty years ago. Abbott et al. in 1981 described a “non-guttata corneal endothelial degeneration” along with changes in the basement membrane (representing DM). His patients showed unilateral spontaneous corneal edema associated with severe attenuation of the endothelium, areas showing total endothelial cell loss and absent guttata. Brooks and his group in 1990 described a non-guttata type of bullous keratopathy in the absence of a relevant history of ocular disease. They described 17 patients with an evidence of bilateral endothelial dysfunction and morphology changes that resulted in corneal decompensation spontaneously either in association with an actual loss of the endothelial cells (in two patients) or without a reduced number of endothelial cells (in four patients). Eleven of those patients showed significant to mild abnormal morphology, represented by pleomorphism and polymegathism. The abnormal morphology was thought to result in increasing the susceptibility of these corneas to surgical stress leading to decompensation following surgery. They proposed this entity as a “non-guttata endothelial dystrophy” based on the bilateral findings in their patients. They also suggested that the significant loss of endothelial cells in some of their patients represented a late advanced stage of the disease. The most recent report found to be informative and interesting is the one presented by Lee and his group 28 years later. They described a similar group of patients with progressive idiopathic unilateral corneal endothelial failure of unknown etiology in phakic eyes. They had comprehensive demonstration of their cases and ruled out other possible causes for unilateral endothelial cell disease and/or loss (congenital hereditary endothelial dystrophy, iridocorneal endothelial syndrome, viral etiology or PEX material). In their histopathological description of eight corneal specimens, they similarly described the intracytoplasmic corneal epithelial swelling, irregularity, and bullous changes, associated with sub-epithelial excess basement membrane in all patients, along with fibrosis in some cases which has been also found in our full thickness PKP corneal specimens. DM of the corneal specimens in their study was thickened and multilayered histopathologically with an endothelial cell loss and absence of guttata. The analysis of transmission electron microscopy showed thick DM and variable deposition of thick collagen layer behind the posterior non-banded zone. Additional features in some patients were also noted which showed re-duplication of banded and non-banded zones of DM. The histopathological and ultrastructural findings in our cases are identical to those described and demonstrated by Lee and his group. The authors considered the pathological condition in their cases as an idiopathic unilateral disease because none of their patients developed similar corneal decompensation in the fellow eye within a follow-up period reaching up to 13 years. It is possible that none of their patients underwent cataract surgery in the fellow eye during that period to demonstrate the early post-operative decompensation observed in our cohort in three patients. This observation and the theory of susceptibility of the abnormal endothelial cells to “surgical stress” that was raised by Brooks in addition to the asymmetrical presentation between the two eyes noted in our cases all support the diagnosis of “non-guttata endothelial dystrophy” in our cohort of patients, that is yet to be further studied genetically. This is also further supported by the status in two of our patients, one who had history of spontaneous corneal decompensation with cataract 6-years earlier in one eye, which was not included in our study (necessitating DSAEK along with the cataract extraction) and another patient who had spontaneous corneal decompensation of the fellow eye 3 years after the first included study eye.

Management of our patients was mainly done by PKP, extracapsular cataract extraction (ECCE) and PC IOL implantation, while other more recent cases underwent DSAEK, phacoemulsification and PC IOL implantation. The success rate of the primary keratoplasty and the repeated grafts (in three patients only) was the same (70%). Price et al. has mentioned that the definitive treatment for corneal endothelial dysfunction is corneal transplant. Quek et al. added that symptomatic corneal edema has been treated successfully with endothelial or PKP. However the most significant risk factor for failure of keratoplasty is prior glaucoma filtration surgery, which accelerates the endothelial cell loss. Similar surgical procedures have been also used for the management of FED cases. PKP and DSAEK are the definitive treatments to restore vision. However, PKP has several potential complications, e.g., significant postoperative astigmatism, prolonged visual recovery, suture trauma, persistent epithelial defects, and recurrent refractive surgery. Therefore, other surgical techniques, such as DSAEK, Descemet’s membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK), posterior lamellar keratoplasty and deep lamellar endothelial keratoplasty are being conducted.

Price et al. reported that 10-year PKP survival rates reach 67–90% in FED patients; however, they get lower in pseudophakic bullous keratopathy (PBK). In one study enrolling > 1000 PKP recipients for endothelial dysfunction, survival rates at ten years were 80% in Fuch’s dystrophy while they reached 63% in patients with PBK and aphakia. Annette J et al.. were able to achieve the best corrected acuity of 20/40 in 37.5% after observing around 100 patients post triple procedures of PKP, ECCE and IOL implantation: 44 were FED patients and the others involved different pathologies like trauma, herpes and other endothelial dystrophy. Interestingly six patients were labelled as “non-guttata corneal endothelial dystrophy”. DMEK and DSAEK alone or with phacoemulsification have been adapted recently to treat such cases due to the documented advantages and safety profile. Success rate in one study of > 2000 patients of FED who underwent DMEK and DSAEK procedures reached more than 90% at 5 years in both groups. Interestingly, it has been documented in some studies that the rate of endothelial cell loss between PKP and DSEAK is similar at 10 years with 78% and 71%, respectively. Although the success rates have varied among different interventions and studies yet, outcomes are generally good. The most common causes for PKP failure are endothelial decompensation following an immunologic rejection episode and late endothelial decompensation.

The limitations of our study include the retrospective nature and the inability to perform specular microscopy testing for all patients because of the poor view. However, based on the previous studies that are suggestive of a non-guttata endothelial dystrophy (or degeneration) and the similar identical findings in our cases, we strongly propose further efforts in studying this entity genetically in our Saudi population.

Conclusions

In conclusion, we provide valuable observations of a “non-guttata endothelial dystrophy” among Saudis that seems to manifest asymmetrically in both eyes with relatively late onset of presentation in the late sixties or older. The endothelial cell count in these cases might or might not be low at presentation in both eyes as previously reported, but it seems to be vulnerable to even uncomplicated surgical stress, thus presenting with decompensation soon after surgery. DM in these cases has a peculiar thickened multi-laminated or multilayered appearance with no evidence of guttata. The need to recognize it in the first affected eye at initial presentation is significant to have a better management plan for the fellow eye when a patient develops cataract, keeping in mind that post-operative corneal decompensation is expected and inevitable soon after surgery. Such patients might be better treated by primary combined procedure since the definitive treatment for the corneal endothelial dysfunction in general is keratoplasty. This management plan may save patients from the morbidity of undergoing two separate surgical interventions. This condition is not well-established and is worth further investigations. There is a remote possibility of this entity being a non-guttata FED variant, thus a careful selection of patients for further genetic testing screening for FED might be useful.

[CONCLUSION]
In conclusion, we provide valuable observations of a “non-guttata endothelial dystrophy” among Saudis that seems to manifest asymmetrically in both eyes with relatively late onset of presentation in the late sixties or older. The endothelial cell count in these cases might or might not be low at presentation in both eyes as previously reported, but it seems to be vulnerable to even uncomplicated surgical stress, thus presenting with decompensation soon after surgery. DM in these cases has a peculiar thickened multi-laminated or multilayered appearance with no evidence of guttata. The need to recognize it in the first affected eye at initial presentation is significant to have a better management plan for the fellow eye when a patient develops cataract, keeping in mind that post-operative corneal decompensation is expected and inevitable soon after surgery. Such patients might be better treated by primary combined procedure since the definitive treatment for the corneal endothelial dysfunction in general is keratoplasty. This management plan may save patients from the morbidity of undergoing two separate surgical interventions. This condition is not well-established and is worth further investigations. There is a remote possibility of this entity being a non-guttata FED variant, thus a careful selection of patients for further genetic testing screening for FED might be useful.
